title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A parliamentary justice committee has commissioned a report on the effectiveness of rehabilitation programmes within the prison system. Your report should identify which types of programmes have the strongest evidence base, explain the barriers to their implementation, and recommend changes to policy or resource allocation.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on the Effectiveness of Prison Rehabilitation Programmes**

**Introduction**
This report reviews the evidence base for rehabilitation programmes in custodial settings, identifies systemic barriers to their effective delivery, and presents recommendations for policymakers. Findings are drawn from a review of Ministry of Justice reoffending data, academic literature and inspectorate reports published over the past decade.

**Most Effective Programme Types**
Three categories of intervention demonstrate consistently strong evidence of reducing reoffending. Accredited cognitive-behavioural programmes, which address offending-related thinking and decision-making, are associated with reoffending reductions of between ten and twenty per cent. Vocational education and skills training improve employment prospects upon release — one of the strongest predictors of desistance from crime. Substance misuse treatment is particularly impactful given that a significant proportion of the prison population committed offences directly related to addiction.

**Barriers to Implementation**
Despite the evidence, delivery is severely constrained by three interrelated factors: chronic overcrowding, which reduces the time prisoners spend in education or work; a shortage of qualified programme facilitators; and inconsistent commissioning that disrupts programme continuity when individuals are transferred between establishments.

**Recommendations**
The committee is invited to consider three policy changes: establishing a statutory minimum entitlement for all eligible prisoners to complete at least one accredited programme; ringfencing rehabilitation budgets against general cost pressures; and piloting dedicated rehabilitation hubs modelled on Scandinavian open-prison principles.

**Conclusion**
Investment in evidence-based rehabilitation is demonstrably more cost-effective than the cycle of reimprisonment that the current system perpetuates.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You have read a newspaper article in which a prominent politician argued that prisons should focus exclusively on punishment and that rehabilitation programmes are a waste of taxpayers' money. Write a formal letter to the editor of the newspaper responding to this argument, presenting evidence to counter the politician's position and explaining the long-term social and economic benefits of effective rehabilitation.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I write in response to the article published in your edition of 14th March, in which the Right Honourable Geoffrey Hardwick argued that rehabilitation programmes represent an unjustifiable use of public funds and that prison should serve exclusively as a punitive institution.

While I appreciate that this position resonates with many members of the public, the evidence does not support it. The Ministry of Justice's own figures show that over fifty per cent of short-sentence prisoners are reconvicted within twelve months of release. Each reoffending incident carries costs to the criminal justice system, to victims and to the wider community. If punishment alone were effective in deterring reoffending, these figures would look considerably different after decades of predominantly punitive policy.

By contrast, prisons that invest seriously in education, vocational training and cognitive-behavioural programmes have consistently achieved lower reoffending rates. Norway, which operates a frankly rehabilitative prison model, has a reoffending rate of approximately twenty per cent — less than half the figure recorded in England and Wales. This is not sentimentality; it is fiscally rational policy.

Mr Hardwick's framing presents punishment and rehabilitation as opposing values, when in reality they are complementary aims. Society is entitled to expect that offenders face meaningful consequences for their actions and that every reasonable step is taken to ensure that those individuals do not create further victims upon release.

I trust your publication will continue to provide space for evidence-based contributions to this important debate.

Yours faithfully,
Dr Amara Okonkwo
Criminology Research Fellow, University of Westbridge

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a non-fiction book called 'Unlocked: Inside the Reform of British Prisons' which examines case studies of rehabilitation initiatives, interviews prisoners and former prisoners, and evaluates the political obstacles to systemic change. Write a review for a social policy journal, assessing the book's research quality, its narrative approach, and the strength of its arguments for reform.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Rigorous, Humane and Urgently Necessary: A Review of 'Unlocked: Inside the Reform of British Prisons'**

Sarah Caldwell's 'Unlocked' is the kind of book that policy debates on criminal justice frequently cite but rarely embody: simultaneously rigorous in its engagement with evidence and genuinely humane in its treatment of the individuals whose lives form its empirical foundation.

Caldwell, a former prison inspector, spent three years interviewing prisoners, prison officers, governors, and reform advocates across fifteen establishments. The result is a richly textured account that grounds statistical arguments in lived experience without sacrificing analytical depth. The case studies are particularly well chosen, illustrating the transformative potential of serious rehabilitation investment — and, equally importantly, the systemic reasons why that potential is so rarely realised at scale.

The book's treatment of political obstacles to reform is its most valuable contribution for specialist readers. Caldwell identifies with precision the electoral and media dynamics that consistently push politicians towards punitive rhetoric and away from evidence-based policy, even when they privately acknowledge the research. This analysis is refreshingly unsentimental and will resonate with anyone who has worked at the interface of criminal justice research and public policy.

If there is a weakness, it lies in the final chapter's recommendations, which, while sensible, are familiar to anyone versed in the literature. Readers hoping for genuinely novel proposals may find the conclusion somewhat predictable.

Nevertheless, 'Unlocked' is essential reading for students of criminal justice, social policy and anyone genuinely interested in whether prison can be made to work. Highly recommended.
